posts.name: Andrew Johnstone
posts.cooked:
I’m most familiar with Pierre Péters Les Chétillons but also have tasted Pierre Moncuit and Pertois Lebrun wines ostensibly from the same vineyard.

But it feels like there’s a been proliferation of other Les Chétillons lately, including Elise Bougy and Adrien Dhondt, although both producers name their wines “Chétillons de Haut,” which makes me wonder if they are the real mccoy.

Looking at CT, I see listings by other winemakers about which I know absolutely nothing — Guy Charlemagne, Christophe Pitois, J.L. Vergnon, and Perseval Farge?!?! (I mean, I recognize the winemakers just not that they make Les Chétillons.)

And this morning Tom Hewson emailed some less-famous Champagne producers to check out at Wine Expo in Paris this February, which included a photo of Marie Doyard holding an André Jacquart 2015 Chétillon [sic?].

Just how many Champanges truly are single vineyard wines from Les Chétillons?

IMG_08202912×3883 1.27 MB

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Les Chétillons is 20 hectares, so just under twice the size of Bâtard-Montrachet (11ha) for example, or a bit less than half the size of the Clos de Vougeot (50ha). So you could be looking at over 200,000 bottles in a big vintage.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked: @William_Kelley thanks for the facts! I didn’t realize the vineyard was so large - makes sense for producers who have a slice of it to leverage the brand. But - is Chetillons a great vineyard? Or is just Peters’ slice special? Or is it all producer?

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked: James Suckling did a big Chetillons tasting of each producer that does a Chetillons bottling (I think they didn’t have Adrien Dhond’s because it was a negoce produced only twice) at Pierre Peters that Rodolphe was kind enough to let us taste through last summer the day after. It’s a very big vineyard, as William noted, and has a lot of producers including de Sousa (which bottling is a bit of a rare bird), Guy Charlemagne, Elise Bougy, etc. Photo below is with some of the lineup, and I think Suckling wrote up a report about it (which I have not read).

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: Though Elise’s two purple labelled bottles aren’t from Chetillons…

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Russell_Faulkner:

Though Elise’s two purple labelled bottles aren’t from Chetillons…

Rodolphe poured us a few of her other wines that were not from Chetillons as well, yes.

posts.name: Andrew Johnstone
posts.cooked: Ooh, very nice. Thank you for this information!

posts.name: Andrew Johnstone
posts.cooked: If anyone can point to the Suckling report Greg mentions, please let me know. I’m not yet a subscriber, but I’d sign up or pay to read that. (I googled but couldn’t find a report from that tasting.)

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Both Chetillon (20 ha) and Chetillon de Haut (15 ha) are large vineyards that neighbor each other. D9 divides them with Chetillon on the east side of the road and Chetillon de Haut on the west side of the road. They really are separate vineyards though many will refer to both as Chetillon and bottle wine made from both as Chetillons even if that isn’t exactly true. It is not uncommon for vineyards divided by a road to have a different names on each side of the road. Both of these vineyards have plots that are better than others and some winemakers do better than others.

Since Peters started putting Chetillon on the label of his Cuvee Speciale and receiving a good deal of attention, many producers have started to bottle Chetillon alone or point out that their wine is also pure Chetillon. Many producers also make Chetillon a key part of their blend such as Taittinger with Comtes de Champagne. With the vineyards being so large, they each have 100s of individual blocks. This means lot of different owners in these vineyards.

I don’t know all the producers who make a Chetillon as some have done it only for a limited/special release.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked: I have also been told that by saying “Les Chetillons”, you can refer to both the Chetillon and Chetillon de Haut vineyards. That makes sense to me as both vineyards have a base of Chetillon in their name. So, in theory, “Les Chetillons” could be a blend of both vineyards and some producers do use “Les Chetillons” in this way. I’ve seen this done in other villages in Champagne as well where you have a few vineyards all near each other where one has a base name and then others have the same base name plus an adjunct like bas, haut, midi, levant, etc…

posts.name: TK Ling
posts.cooked: We asked Rodolphe when we had lunch with him, who were the producers of Cheitllons and he listed 8, Robert Moncuit, Pierre Moncuit, de Sousa, Elise Bougy, Pertois-Lebrun, Guy Charlemagne, Andre Jacquart and Pierre Peters.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: As you can see here from the cadastral subdivisions, there are quite a few owners (sure, some producers surely own multiple subdivisions, but it gives an idea nonetheless): Carte interactive | cadastre.data.gouv.fr

posts.name: Andrew Johnstone
posts.cooked:
Emailed Suckling, and his team pointed me to the note about the tasting of Les Chétillons, which is brief but contains some interesting nuggets.
